title: Quantum work beyond classical (commuting) limits
authors: Sumit Rout, Aravinth Balaji Ravichandran, Paweł Horodecki, Anubhav Chaturvedi
abstract: Free energy fixes the maximum work of a thermodynamic process once the state and Hamiltonian are specified. A work-extraction task asks a different question: how much average work can a single device realize across several preparations and Hamiltonian settings? A classical work device is one whose Hamiltonian settings are mutually commuting. We place every branch at its best free-energy-limited work envelope and derive the corresponding classical limit on the task average. For pure preparations, the source is specified only by pairwise maximal-energy constraints: for each pair, the intrinsic maximal average energy under one common normalized Hamiltonian is bounded as part of the task data, while the work device is otherwise microscopically unrestricted. The benchmark is optimized over arbitrary-dimensional classical implementations. Incompatible Hamiltonian settings exceed this limit, even though every branch remains bounded by its own free-energy maximum. The advantage therefore does not arise in any single process, but in the average work of the task: incompatible Hamiltonians realize a value that no classical work device can attain. Hamiltonian incompatibility is thus a thermodynamic resource for work extraction.
summary: The paper proves that non-commuting Hamiltonian settings allow for a higher average work extraction across multiple processes than any possible commuting (classical) device. It establishes a new thermodynamic law and identifies Hamiltonian incompatibility as a fundamental resource for work extraction.
main_problem: Determining whether Hamiltonian incompatibility (non-commutativity) acts as a thermodynamic resource to extract more average work across multiple tasks than any classical (commuting) device can achieve.
main_result: The authors derive a universal classical average-work law and prove that incompatible Hamiltonians can exceed this limit, establishing Hamiltonian incompatibility as a fundamental thermodynamic resource.
method: The study uses a thermodynamic framework involving nonequilibrium free-energy differences, quantum relative entropy, and geometric optimization over tracial noncommuting polynomials and Bloch-sphere geometries.
model_or_system: A work-extraction task involving a black-box device queried with bounded Hamiltonians (0 <= H <= I) using a source of pure qubit preparations constrained by pairwise maximal-energy data.
key_observables: Average work (W_p), average-work advantage (Delta_av), and visibility (v).
important_parameters: Hamiltonian commutativity, visibility threshold (v_c), source geometry (theta), and reference energy averages (mu_j).
main_assumptions: The preparations are assumed to be pure, and the work device is treated as a black box with no specific microscopic model or dimension restriction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the work-extraction task, showing the flow from the constrained source to the work device and comparing the free-energy envelope to the commutativity constraint.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the work-extraction task, establishes the branch-wise free-energy benchmark, derives the classical average-work law for a minimal task, quantifies the quantum advantage, and analyzes the robustness of this advantage against noise and task complexity.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and thermodynamics as it identifies a new way to certify quantum advantage using thermodynamic quantities rather than Bell inequalities, and treats non-commutativity as a quantifiable resource.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Work-extraction task. A pure preparation |ψx⟩is supplied to a single work device, while the source side is constrained only by pairwise maximal-energy constraints under one common normalized Hamiltonian. The device is queried through a bounded Hamiltonian setting 0 ≤Hy ≤I and may use an arbitrary thermodynamic protocol involving a heat bath, internal control, and work storage. Each run realizes one branch of the task, and repeated runs define the prior-weighted task average. Classicality is imposed only by requiring the Hamiltonian settings of the same device to commute.
